audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Sanhedrin conflict, resurrection hope, Lord's encouragement, assassination plot, nephew warning, transfer letter to Felix.

verses.restored: Looking intently at the council, Paul said, Men, siblings, I have conducted myself in all good conscience before the Divine until this day.
The high priest Ananias commanded those standing by him to strike his mouth.
Then Paul said to him, The Divine is about to strike you, whitewashed wall. You sit judging me according to the Law, and violating the Law, command me to be struck?
Those standing by said, Do you insult the high priest of the Divine?
Paul said, I did not know, siblings, that he is high priest; for it is written, You shall not speak harmfully of a ruler of your people.

Knowing that one part were Sadducees and the other Pharisees, Paul cried out in the council, Men, siblings, I am a Pharisee, son of Pharisees; concerning hope and resurrection of the dead I am judged.
When he said this, a dispute happened between Pharisees and Sadducees, and the multitude was divided.
For Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor messenger, nor spirit; but Pharisees confess both.
A great cry arose, and some scribes of the party of the Pharisees stood and argued strongly, saying, We find nothing harmful in this human. What if a spirit or messenger spoke to him?
When a great dispute happened, the commander, fearing Paul might be torn apart by them, commanded the soldiers to go down, snatch him from their midst, and bring him into the barracks.
The following night the Lord stood by him and said, Take courage, for as you bore witness concerning me in Jerusalem, so it is necessary also for you to bear witness in Rome.
When day came, the Judeans made a conspiracy and bound themselves with an oath, saying neither to eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.
There were more than forty who made this sworn plot.
They came to the chief priests and elders and said, We have bound ourselves with a curse to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.

Now therefore you, with the council, make clear to the commander that he should bring him down to you, as though you are about to examine more accurately the things about him; we are ready to kill him before he draws near.
But the son of Paul's sister heard of the ambush, and coming and entering the barracks, reported it to Paul.
Paul called one of the centurions and said, Bring this young man to the commander, for he has something to report to him.
Taking him, he brought him to the commander and says, The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you, because he has something to say to you.
The commander took him by the hand, withdrew privately, and inquired, What is it that you have to report to me?
He said, The Judeans agreed to ask you to bring Paul down tomorrow to the council, as though intending to inquire something more accurately about him.

Therefore do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty of their men lie in ambush for him, having bound themselves by oath neither to eat nor drink until they have killed him; and now they are ready, awaiting the promise from you.
The commander therefore released the young man, commanding him, Tell no one that you reported these things to me.
Calling two of the centurions, he said, Prepare two hundred soldiers to go as far as Caesarea, seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen, from the third hour of the night;
also provide mounts so that, setting Paul upon them, they may bring him safely to Felix the governor.
He wrote a letter having this form:
Claudius Lysias to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings.
This man was seized by the Judeans and was about to be killed by them when I came upon them with the troops and rescued him, having learned that he is Roman.
Wishing to know the accusation for which they were accusing him, I brought him down to their council.
I found him accused concerning questions of their Law, but having no accusation worthy of death or bonds.
When it was disclosed to me that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you immediately, also commanding the accusers to speak the things against him before you.
The soldiers therefore, according to what was ordered them, taking Paul, brought him by night to Antipatris.
The next day, allowing the horsemen to go on with him, they returned to the barracks.
Those entering Caesarea and delivering the letter to the governor also presented Paul to him.
Reading it and asking from what province he was, learning that he was from Cilicia,
he said, I will hear you fully when your accusers also arrive. He commanded him to be guarded in Herod's praetorium.
